subUnits.title: 5.1 Factors Affecting Rate of Chemical Reactions
notes: Rate of Reaction: Change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time (mol dm⁻³ s⁻¹).
According to Collision Theory, reaction rate depends on frequency and effectiveness of particle collisions.
1. Nature of Reactants:
• Ionic compounds react faster than covalent compounds
• State of division: powders react faster than lumps (larger surface area)
• Reactivity series: more reactive metals react faster with acids
2. Concentration/Pressure:
• Higher concentration → more particles per unit volume → more frequent collisions → faster rate
• For gases, increasing pressure has the same effect as increasing concentration
3. Temperature:
• Higher temperature → particles gain kinetic energy → move faster → more collisions
• More particles possess energy ≥ Activation Energy (Ea)
• Rule of thumb: Rate ≈ doubles for every 10°C rise
4. Catalyst:
• Substance that increases rate without being consumed
• Provides alternative reaction pathway with lower Ea
• Does not shift equilibrium or change ΔH
• Examples: MnO₂ in H₂O₂ decomposition, Fe in Haber process, enzymes in biology
Mathematical Expression (Introductory):
Rate ∝ [Reactants]ⁿ (n = order of reaction, determined experimentally)
Practical Applications:
• Refrigeration slows food spoilage (lowers temperature)
• Catalysts in catalytic converters reduce vehicle emissions
• Pressure used in industrial ammonia production (Haber process)
diagramDescriptions: Energy distribution graph (Maxwell-Boltzmann) showing fraction of molecules with E ≥ Ea at T1 and T2
Diagram comparing lump vs powdered solid reacting with acid, showing gas evolution rate
Catalyzed vs uncatalyzed reaction pathway on energy profile diagram
Collision frequency illustration showing high vs low concentration particles
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Factors affecting rate of reaction chemistry WAEC JAMB site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Increasing the concentration of reactants increases reaction rate because:
quiz.explanation: Higher concentration means more particles per unit volume, leading to more frequent and effective collisions.

quiz.q: A catalyst increases reaction rate by:
quiz.explanation: Catalysts provide an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy barrier.

quiz.q: For gaseous reactions, increasing pressure is equivalent to increasing:
quiz.explanation: Higher pressure forces gas particles closer together, effectively increasing concentration and collision frequency.

quiz.q: Powdered calcium carbonate reacts faster with HCl than lumps because it has:
quiz.explanation: Powdered solids expose more particles to the reactant, increasing the frequency of effective collisions.

quiz.q: Which statement about temperature and reaction rate is TRUE?
opts: Rate approximately doubles for every 10°C rise
quiz.explanation: Higher temperature increases kinetic energy and the fraction of molecules with E ≥ Ea, roughly doubling the rate per 10°C.

quiz.q: A catalyst does NOT affect:
quiz.explanation: Catalysts speed up both forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium position and yield remain unchanged.

quiz.q: Ionic reactions are generally faster than covalent reactions because:
opts: No bonds need to be broken, only electrostatic attraction occurs
quiz.explanation: Ionic reactions involve attraction between oppositely charged ions, while covalent reactions require bond breaking.

quiz.q: Refrigerating food slows down spoilage by:
opts: Lowering temperature and reducing reaction rate
quiz.explanation: Lower temperatures reduce molecular kinetic energy, decreasing collision frequency and rate of decomposition/spoilage.

quiz.q: In the decomposition of H₂O₂, MnO₂ acts as a:
quiz.explanation: MnO₂ speeds up H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂ without being consumed, fitting the definition of a catalyst.

quiz.q: Which factor would LEAST affect the rate of a reaction in aqueous solution?
quiz.explanation: Liquids and solids are nearly incompressible, so pressure changes have negligible effect on reaction rates in solution.

subUnits.title: 5.2 Types of Chemical Reactions: Endothermic & Exothermic
notes: Chemical reactions involve energy changes due to bond breaking (requires energy) and bond making (releases energy).
Exothermic Reactions:
• Release heat to surroundings (ΔH is NEGATIVE)
• Temperature of surroundings increases
• Energy of products < Energy of reactants
• Examples: Combustion, neutralization, respiration, condensation, freezing
• Equation: Reactants → Products + Heat (ΔH = -ve)
Endothermic Reactions:
• Absorb heat from surroundings (ΔH is POSITIVE)
• Temperature of surroundings decreases
• Energy of products > Energy of reactants
• Examples: Photosynthesis, thermal decomposition, evaporation, dissolving NH₄NO₃ in water
• Equation: Reactants + Heat → Products (ΔH = +ve)
Enthalpy Change (ΔH):
ΔH = ΣH(products) - ΣH(reactants)
• Measured in kJ/mol or J/mol
• Determined experimentally using calorimetry
Energy Profile Diagrams:
• X-axis: Reaction progress, Y-axis: Potential energy
• Exothermic: Products at lower energy level than reactants
• Endothermic: Products at higher energy level than reactants
• Activation energy (Ea) always positive for both types
Practical Significance:
• Exothermic: Used in hand warmers, self-heating cans, thermite welding
• Endothermic: Used in cold packs, baking (heat absorption for rising), photosynthesis for food production
diagramDescriptions: Energy profile diagram for exothermic reaction showing ΔH < 0, reactants higher than products
Energy profile diagram for endothermic reaction showing ΔH > 0, products higher than reactants
Calorimetry setup diagram: insulated cup, thermometer, stirrer, reactants
Comparison table: exothermic vs endothermic with examples, ΔH sign, and temperature change
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Endothermic exothermic reactions enthalpy chemistry WAEC JAMB site:youtube.com

quiz.q: In an exothermic reaction, the enthalpy change (ΔH) is:
quiz.explanation: Exothermic reactions release heat, so products have lower energy than reactants, making ΔH negative.

quiz.q: Which process is endothermic?
quiz.explanation: Photosynthesis absorbs solar energy to convert CO₂ and H₂O into glucose, making it endothermic.

quiz.q: In an endothermic reaction, energy is:
quiz.explanation: Endothermic reactions require heat input, so energy is absorbed from the surroundings.

quiz.q: The enthalpy change ΔH is calculated as:
quiz.explanation: ΔH = ΣH(products) - ΣH(reactants). Positive ΔH = endothermic, negative ΔH = exothermic.

quiz.q: Which reaction typically releases the most heat?
quiz.explanation: Combustion and metal oxidation are highly exothermic; burning Mg releases intense heat and light.

quiz.q: In a cold pack used for injuries, the reaction is:
quiz.explanation: Cold packs use endothermic dissolution (e.g., NH₄NO₃ in water) to absorb heat and cool the area.

quiz.q: Activation energy is required for:
quiz.explanation: All chemical reactions require activation energy to break initial bonds and reach the transition state.

quiz.q: Which statement about neutralization is TRUE?
opts: It is exothermic with ΔH ≈ -57 kJ/mol
quiz.explanation: Acid-base neutralization is consistently exothermic, releasing approximately 57 kJ per mole of H₂O formed.

quiz.q: In an energy profile diagram, the peak represents:
quiz.explanation: The peak is the highest energy point where bonds are partially broken/formed (transition state).

quiz.q: Thermal decomposition of calcium carbonate is endothermic because:
opts: It requires continuous heat to proceed
quiz.explanation: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ requires continuous heating to break bonds, indicating ΔH > 0 (endothermic).
